class Solution {
public:
    int mostFrequentEven(vector<int>& nums) {
        unordered_map<int, int> freq;
        for (int num : nums) {
            if (num % 2 == 0) {
                freq[num]++;
            }
        }
        if (freq.empty()) return -1;
        
        int max_count = 0;
        for (const auto& p : freq) {
            if (p.second > max_count) {
                max_count = p.second;
            }
        }
        
        int min_num = INT_MAX;
        for (const auto& p : freq) {
            if (p.second == max_count) {
                if (p.first < min_num) {
                    min_num = p.first;
                }
            }
        }
        return min_num;
    }
};

solution: 
#include <unordered_map>
#include <climits>
using namespace std;

int mostFrequentEven(vector<int>& nums) {
    unordered_map<int, int> freq;
    for (int num : nums) {
        if (num % 2 == 0) {
            freq[num]++;
        }
    }
    if (freq.empty()) return -1;
    
    int max_count = 0;
    for (const auto& p : freq) {
        if (p.second > max_count) {
            max_count = p.second;
        }
    }
    
    int min_num = INT_MAX;
    for (const auto& p : freq) {
        if (p.second == max_count && p.first < min_num) {
            min_num = p.first;
        }
    }
    return min_num;
}

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: Uses a frequency map to track even elements' counts. Finds the maximum frequency and the smallest element among those with maximum frequency. Linear time and space, optimal for constraints.
